artifacts.content:
Hi Mark,

I wanted to flag what happened yesterday afternoon walking home with Sofia and how it’s shaping the way I’m looking at the Fourth Street corridor.

On our walk back from Santa Rosa Middle School yesterday (10/18/22), Sofia and I were almost hit in the eastbound crosswalk at Fourth & D around 4:30 p.m. The driver rolled through on a right-on-red while looking left for gaps, never once scanning the crosswalk. I had Sofia’s hand and we had the WALK, but I had to yank her back onto the curb. She was shaking all the way to Humboldt.

It really drove home how exposed people feel on that three‑block stretch between B and E. The crossing distances at 4th & D and 4th & E are too long, the existing striping is faded, and the corners are so wide that drivers take them fast. Between that, the bus stops, and all the kids cutting through from school, it’s a bad mix.

I know we’ve been talking conceptually about bulb‑outs, high‑visibility crosswalk striping, and ADA ramps as part of the Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II project along Fourth Street between B Street and E Street. After yesterday, I’d really like to prioritize those elements in this segment and make sure the curb extensions at 4th & D and 4th & E don’t get value‑engineered out.

Happy to walk the corridor with you one afternoon so you can see what it feels like at school let‑out.

Laura

Laura M. Hernandez
Senior Planner, City of Santa Rosa Planning and Economic Development Department
100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 3
(707) 543-3200

metadata.subject: Fourth St safety concerns after yesterday’s incident with Sofia

artifacts.content:
Hi Angela,

Thanks again for sending over the collision reports for Fourth Street between B and E. I went through the Jan 2020–Dec 2022 data last night, and the pattern at 4th & E is pretty stark.

Those three pedestrian injury cases at the Fourth & E Street intersection, especially the one involving the 72‑year‑old woman leaving the evening diabetes support group at Kaiser Permanente Santa Rosa Medical Center who fractured her hip crossing toward the bus stop on Fourth, really underscore what we’re seeing anecdotally. Between that, my own near‑miss with Sofia at 4th & D, and the complaints we hear during downtown events, it’s clear this corridor is doing a lot of work for vulnerable users.

We’re folding this into the Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II project that Public Works’ Capital Projects Engineering Division is carrying out in the existing public right‑of‑way. I’m drafting the justification now and leaning heavily on CEQA Guidelines §15301(c) (Existing Facilities) and §15304(h) (Minor Alterations to Land) so we can move quickly on the bulb‑outs, ADA‑compliant curb ramps, and the two curbside bus stop landing pads without getting bogged down in a full Initial Study.

If you have any additional narrative details (time of day, direction of travel) on that older adult from the diabetes group, that would help us describe how the lighting and bus pad improvements will directly benefit seniors and transit riders.

Really appreciate your help on this.

Best,
Laura

Laura M. Hernandez
Senior Planner, Planning & Economic Development
City of Santa Rosa

Hey Danny,

Catching up between meetings and thought I’d answer your “why are you so obsessed with 4th St sidewalks” question.

Ever since Mom fell on that busted sidewalk panel near Fourth & C back in Feb 2021 and had to do eight weeks of PT at Santa Rosa Memorial’s outpatient rehab, I can’t walk that block without seeing every crack. She keeps downplaying it, but you remember how scared she was about losing her balance for good. A lot of her neighbors from the senior apartments near B Street use walkers or canes to get to the Mexican grocery and the pharmacy on Fourth. If one broken panel sent Mom to therapy for two months, imagine what it’s like for someone with even less stability.

So yes, I’ve been pushing hard to get comprehensive panel replacement and pedestrian‑scale LED lighting into the Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II project. On top of that, there’s Sofia’s near‑miss at 4th & D in October 2022 and the 72‑year‑old woman who fractured her hip at 4th & E after leaving a diabetes support group at Kaiser and trying to reach the bus stop. It’s not abstract for me anymore; it’s our family.

The good news is we’ve structured the work so it fits under the CEQA Class 1 (Existing Facilities) and Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land) exemptions—no new lanes, all within existing right‑of‑way—so we can actually build it instead of arguing about paperwork for a year.

Anyway, enough planner ranting. How are your kids doing with science fair season?

Abrazo,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why I’m so focused on 4th St sidewalks

artifacts.content:
Text thread – Laura & Carlos

Date: 2023-09-07

Carlos: Hey, can we switch Thursday’s pickup? I’m still avoiding downtown if I can.

Laura: I figured. Ever since that June 2019 rear‑end at 4th & B you tense up just hearing “downtown.”

Carlos: Yeah, that intersection still makes my stomach drop.

Laura: It’s okay. On the 50/50 weeks when she’s coming from your place in Rohnert Park, Sofia can keep doing the CityBus + walk combo. She knows the route from the stop along Fourth to Humboldt by heart now.

Carlos: I just hate that she has to walk that corridor. Too many close calls.

Laura: Same. That’s why I’m pouring so much into this Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II project. We’re putting in high‑vis crosswalks, curb extensions, and proper bus stop landing pads along Fourth between B and E. It’s literally the stretch she uses on custody‑exchange days.

Carlos: When are they actually building it though?

Laura: If we keep it under the CEQA Class 1 / Class 4 categorical exemptions, we can get construction into June–August 2025 while she’s on summer break. No new lanes, no parking removal, all in existing right‑of‑way, so we avoid a full Initial Study/MND and that 6–9 month delay and $85–100k in consultant costs.

Carlos: That would be huge. I’d feel better if most of the work is done before she starts 8th grade.

Laura: Me too. In the meantime I’ll keep reminding her to text when she gets off the bus and when she hits our block on Humboldt.

Carlos: Deal.

metadata.title: Text thread with Carlos about Sofia’s route

artifacts.content:
Personal project + life notes – Downtown safety + CEQA

Date: 2023-11-03
Location: Desk, 100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 3

– Remember: DÍA DE LOS MUERTOS FEEDBACK.
  • Volunteers last November (2021) said older attendees leaving Courthouse Square around 9:30 p.m. felt unsafe crossing at 4th & E.
  • Near‑miss with vendor carrying the cash box back to her car – almost hit in the dark crosswalk.
  • Aunt Isabel heard it directly and was furious. Use this to justify pedestrian‑scale LED lighting and high‑vis striping in the Fourth & E scope.

– Collisions + family stories all point to same three blocks of Fourth Street between B and E:
  • Sofia near‑miss at 4th & D on 10/18/22.
  • 72‑year‑old woman from Kaiser diabetes support group, hip fracture at 4th & E crossing to bus stop.
  • Mom’s trip‑and‑fall on broken panel near 4th & C in Feb 2021 → eight weeks PT at Memorial outpatient.
  • Carlos still won’t drive downtown after June 2019 rear‑end at 4th & B, so Sofia uses bus + walks that corridor on some custody days.

– CEQA strategy:
  • Keep Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II clearly within Class 1 (Existing Facilities, §15301(c)) + Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land, §15304(h)).
  • No lane reductions, no parking loss (learned from Sonoma Ave road diet backlash May 2022 when Isabel + 2 others threatened to sue Council).
  • All work in existing public right‑of‑way; no capacity increase, no historic resource impacts.

– This is why I’m okay signing the Notice of Exemption as lead agency contact (Laura M. Hernandez, Senior Planner) – it’s defensible and it keeps construction on track for summer 2025 when Sofia’s not walking through a work zone every day.

metadata.title: Personal project + life notes – 4th St corridor

artifacts.content:
Hi Mami,

I’m eating leftover lentil soup at my desk and thinking about you, so I figured I’d write.

Do you remember when you and Papá stayed with me on Humboldt for two weeks during the Glass Fire evacuations in September 2020? The City’s budget has been tight ever since. Karen keeps reminding us we can’t spend $85,000–$100,000 on CEQA consultants for every little project, especially if it means 6–9 months of delay. That’s why I’m being so careful with this downtown pedestrian safety project.

We’re calling it the Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II. It’s just three blocks of Fourth Street between B and E, fixing sidewalks like the broken panel you hit near 4th & C in Feb 2021, adding ADA ramps, better lighting, and safer crosswalks. I’m keeping it under the Class 1 and Class 4 CEQA categorical exemptions so we don’t have to do a big environmental report. No new lanes, no parking removal, just improving what’s already there.

I’m also double‑checking everything with James from the City Attorney’s office after that March 2023 audit where I misclassified the College Avenue infill project and had to write a correction memo. I do not want to repeat that mistake.

The goal is to build most of it June–August 2025 while Sofi is on summer break, so she’s not walking through construction every day between Santa Rosa Middle School and the apartment. Between her near‑miss at 4th & D and your fall, I feel like this corridor is my personal mission.

We’re still on for Sunday in Windsor. Text me your grocery list.

Te quiero,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why this downtown project matters so much

artifacts.content:
Event: File Notice of Exemption – Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II
Date: 2026-01-10
Time: 9:00–10:30 a.m.
Location: Sonoma County Clerk-Recorder-Assessor, 585 Fiscal Drive, Room 103
Attendees: Laura M. Hernandez; courier from Public Works (TBD)

Description/Notes:

– Meet courier in lobby at 9:00 a.m. with signed Notice of Exemption for the Santa Rosa Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II project (Fourth Street between B Street and E Street, including intersections at Fourth & D and Fourth & E, Santa Rosa, CA 95401).

– Confirm document lists:
  • From Public Agency: City of Santa Rosa Planning and Economic Development Department, 100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 3.
  • Project Applicant: City of Santa Rosa Public Works Department.
  • Name of public agency approving project: City of Santa Rosa City Council.
  • Name of person/agency carrying out project: City of Santa Rosa Public Works Department, Capital Projects Engineering Division.
  • Contact Person: Laura M. Hernandez, Senior Planner, (707) 543-3200.
  • CEQA exemptions: Class 1, Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines §15301(c)); Class 4, Minor Alterations to Land (§15304(h)).
  • Reasons summarized: minor alterations to existing public streets/sidewalks; no new travel lanes, no capacity increase; all work in existing right-of-way; no sensitive habitats, hazardous waste sites, or historic resource impacts; no exceptions under §15300.2.

– Double‑check signature and date (1/10/2026) before handing over. This keeps construction on track for summer and finally gets those high‑visibility crosswalks, curb extensions, ADA ramps, sidewalk panel replacements, pedestrian‑scale LED lighting, and bus stop pads built for Sofia, Mom, and everyone else who walks that corridor.

– After filing, call Mark to confirm timestamp and scan receipt into project folder.

metadata.title: Calendar – File NOE for 4th St Pedestrian Safety Phase II

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez@santarosa.gov>
To: Mark Delaney <mark.delaney@santarosa.gov>
Cc: Jenna Price <jenna.price@santarosa.gov>, Kevin Lu <kevin.lu@santarosa.gov>
Date: 2025-03-11 8:42 AM
Subject: CEQA justification – Downtown Ped Safety Phase II / Mendocino crossing

Hi Mark,

I’m drafting the internal memo on why the Mendocino Avenue crosswalk in front of Santa Rosa Middle School can stay within the CEQA Class 1 and 4 categorical exemptions as part of the Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II package, and I want to make sure I’m characterizing our rationale accurately.

One of the reasons I pushed so hard to keep this within Class 1 (existing facilities) and Class 4 (minor alterations) is the October 12, 2023 near‑miss crash at the Mendocino Avenue crosswalk in front of Santa Rosa Middle School, where my daughter, Sofia Hernandez, and her classmate were nearly hit walking home. After that incident, you and I sat down and reworked the design so the curb extensions, high‑visibility striping, and daylighting could clearly qualify as minor alterations to existing streets, rather than a wholesale reconfiguration that might trigger a full EIR.

As we discussed, a full EIR could easily delay construction by 12–18 months, which, in practical terms, means at least another school year (or two) of kids crossing in its current condition. The whole point of the internal redesign was to keep the safety improvements implementable under the Class 1 and 4 exemptions while still meaningfully reducing risk at that crossing.

Can you confirm you’re comfortable with this framing, and flag any technical language you’d like me to tweak before I send the draft to the City Attorney’s office this afternoon?

Thanks,
Laura

Laura Marisol Hernandez
Senior Planner
City of Santa Rosa – Planning & Economic Development
2185 Humboldt Street, Apt 3B
Santa Rosa, CA 95404
Mobile: 707-555-2394

metadata.subject: CEQA justification – Downtown Ped Safety Phase II / Mendocino crossing

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez@santarosa.gov>
To: Elena Ruiz <elenaruiz58@example.com>
Date: 2025-03-15 9:06 PM
Subject: Why I’m so wrapped up in this crosswalk project

Mami,

I know you think I’m working too many late nights again, especially when Sofia is with Carlos, but I want you to understand why I’m pushing this downtown pedestrian safety work so hard.

Ever since the October 12, 2023 incident at the Mendocino Avenue crosswalk in front of Santa Rosa Middle School, I haven’t been able to shake the image of Sofia Hernandez and her little friend frozen in that driver’s headlights. It was technically a “near‑miss crash,” but it felt way too close. That’s why I’ve been so insistent about how we structure the Mendocino improvements and the rest of the Downtown Pedestrian Safety Improvements – Phase II package.

I sat down with our traffic engineer, Mark Delaney, and we went line by line through the design to make sure it stays a minor alteration to existing facilities. If we let it creep beyond what fits under the CEQA Class 1 and 4 categorical exemptions, they’ll make us do a full Environmental Impact Report. That could delay construction by 12–18 months. I can’t accept another year or two of kids, including Sofia, running that gauntlet every afternoon.

So yes, I’m tired, but when I walk that corridor and imagine better lighting and safer crossings for abuelitos and kids, it feels worth it. I promise I’m still coming up Sunday to Windsor with groceries and to check on Papá’s meds.

Te quiero,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why I’m so wrapped up in this crosswalk project
